title: Phase 9 burrow ship V1 targets: fly + docker + tarball (not fly + render)
rationale: The second/third targets exist primarily to prove the ShipTarget interface is genuinely generic — a single implementation can't tell you whether fly-specific assumptions have leaked into the seam. Render was the original second target but the user does not use Render, so it would be untested code with weak dogfooding. fly + docker + tarball was chosen instead because: (1) tarball stresses interface SHAPE (sync, no network, no auth, no streaming) — catches leaky abstractions; (2) docker stresses interface LIFECYCLE (async, streaming build logs as events, long-running, can fail mid-stream) — catches event-model gaps; (3) fly is the real-world dogfood target and naturally COMPOSES the docker target (build image → push → flyctl deploy), so making docker a peer rather than fly-internal forces the docker-build primitive to be factored out cleanly. Docker is explicitly user-facing so 'burrow ship --target docker' is supported standalone (handing an image to a colleague, pushing to a private registry, running locally) — not just a means to fly. Fly credentials are NOT required to implement Phase 9; the target shells out to flyctl and assumes host-level auth, with --dry-run for validation without provider calls.

name: ship-target-event-stream-seam
description: ShipTarget interface (src/ship/target.ts) is the load-bearing seam for burrow ship. Each target implements: validate(ctx), installCheck(ctx), plan(ctx), execute(ctx)→AsyncGenerator<ShipEvent>. ShipEvent is a discriminated union (plan|step.start|step.stdout|step.stderr|step.end|artifact|done) so the CLI can stream NDJSON or render pretty without target-specific code. Three V1 built-ins (tarball, docker, fly) prove the seam: tarball=sync+offline+no-auth, docker=streaming long-running build, fly=composes docker for real-world deploy. Adding a new target = create src/ship/targets/<id>.ts, register in registry.ts. Lifecycle invariant: execute() yields exactly one done event last on success; on AbortSignal it cleans up and returns without done.

content: [ship].default_target in burrow.toml is schema-locked to the three V1 built-ins (tarball|docker|fly). Custom ShipTargets registered via ShipRegistry.register() are reachable only via the --target CLI flag, NOT via default_target. Default_target lives on disk and gets shared across the team, so pinning it to known-good targets keeps the contract small. Tests for custom targets must pass options.target rather than writing default_target into burrow.toml.

name: shared-runStep-helper
description: src/ship/run.ts hosts probeBinary() (which-on-PATH lookup), streamLines() (Uint8Array→line-by-line decoder that flushes the final partial line), and runStep() (Bun.spawn → AsyncGenerator<step.start|step.stdout|step.stderr|step.end>). All three V1 ship targets reach for runStep instead of writing their own spawn+line-buffer code; the merged stdout/stderr stream interleaves lines fairly via Promise.race over per-iterator next() calls. Cancellation: runStep wires the input AbortSignal to proc.kill() with once:true. Tests stub spawn via the spawn? param.

content: burrow + warren coordinated multi-worker release pairs burrow 0.3.0 (pl-cb3e, burrow-side serve substrate: --bind-host, /admin/drain, GET /burrows/:id/files, multi-worker DEPLOY.md) with warren 0.3.0 (pl-9ba1, warren-side placement substrate: workers/burrows tables, BurrowClientPool). The two releases are sibling-plans and ship same day; CHANGELOG headlines on each cite the parent plan + parent seed (burrow-62ce on burrow side, warren-6747 on warren side) so the cross-repo coupling is recoverable from git alone.

Release flow (.claude/commands/release.md): version:bump patch, move CHANGELOG [Unreleased]→new version dated today, then commit 'release: vX.Y.Z'. gh CLI is unavailable in the burrow sandbox so GH issue closure must be done host-side; capture issue credit in the commit + CHANGELOG instead.
